skeptic — devils-advocate critique of every hypothesis (gad-credibility)

User directive: "we need devils advocate to our theory/hypothesis
and how we can improve to be more credible."

Ships:
- .planning/SKEPTIC.md: comprehensive critique of every claim
  the project has made, structured as steelman → problems →
  alternatives → falsification conditions → honest current
  status. Covers freedom hypothesis, compound-skills hypothesis,
  emergent-evolution hypothesis, pressure-as-measurable, and
  GAD's value prop. Plus six cross-cutting methodology critiques
  (sample size, no randomization, judge=architect, post-hoc
  hypotheses, single domain, rubric drift). Ten ranked moves
  that would make us more credible — top three are pre-registr-
  ation for round 5, replicate runs per condition, and a true
  ablation arm.
- /skeptic page rendering essentially the same content, color-
  coded (rose=problems, amber=alternatives, sky=falsification,
  emerald=improvements). Public commitment to admitting what
  we don't know.
- Nav: Theory dropdown gains Skeptic link.
- Hero: rose-bordered "honest disclosure" callout linking to
  /skeptic, stating N=2-5 runs per condition, one human
  reviewer, one task domain. Reads as exploratory observation
  not tested claims.

The credibility move is admitting what we don't know. The
right word for what we're doing is exploratory analysis, not
hypothesis testing. /skeptic exists to make that explicit
to every reader.

EOF

  existing skills (portfolio-sync literally describes the

Fixes shipped this commit:
- skills/session-discipline/SKILL.md — hard-start checklist
  every agent turn must follow: snapshot → registry check →
  skill scan → adopt task → write skills as patterns emerge →
  update registry → update STATE → commit with task id → push.
  Load first at every session start.
- gad-78 decision: dogfood failure captured formally as a
  finding against CSH (skills don't compound if not invoked).
- gad-79 decision: task_pressure operationalized programmatically
  per user answer to pressure-score-formula open question.
  Formula: raw = R + 2*G + C; score = log2(raw+1) / log2(65).
  R = <requirement> count, G = <gate> count, C = amends cross-
  cuts. Computed at prebuild from REQUIREMENTS.xml structure.
  No human override, no <pressure-profile> block, no subjective
  sub-dimensions. Distinct from game_pressure (in-game player
  experience concept) — naming disambiguated.

Prebuild:
- computeTaskPressure(xml) walks REQUIREMENTS.xml counting R/G/C
  and computes normalized score. v5 result: R=21 G=4 C=10 raw=39
  score=0.884. Emitted as TASK_PRESSURE[version] typed export.

/roadmap:

  helper reading computed values from TASK_PRESSURE. Rounds 4-5
  show "computed" badge; rounds 1-3 stay "authored" (pre-v4
  templates not preserved).
- Data-provenance caption updated to show formula + source fields.
- source badge on each round entry in the pressure progression.

Q1 resolved: programmatic structural pressure.
Q2 resolved: metadata only, stored in TRACE (not a rubric dim).

22-46 22-47: skill findability investigation + /standards page + gad-80..84

User directive 2026-04-09: "are [emergent skills] findable in our
system? can claude code or codex use them if installed using gad?
we need to reference [Anthropic PDF + agentskills.io] a lot
throughout our site."

Honest answer to the findability question (new doc
.planning/docs/SKILL-FINDABILITY-2026-04-09.md):

- gad install does NOT copy the skills/ directory at all. It
  installs commands/gad/*.md as slash-command skills + agents +
  a single bundled skill. Workspace skills (create-skill,
  portfolio-sync, session-discipline, etc.) are ONLY usable by
  agents working inside the GAD repo itself.
- Our skills live at skills/ (repo root) — neither the
  Claude-native .claude/skills/ path nor the cross-client
  .agents/skills/ convention.
- The excluded-from-default-install:true flag I added to the
  emergent skill last turn is currently COSMETIC because the
  installer doesn't even read workspace skills.
- This is a gap for fundamentals like the gad-73 triumvirate
  (create-skill / merge-skill / find-skills) which SHOULD be
  installable to user workspaces.

5 new decisions captured:
- gad-80: Adopt agentskills.io standard — .agents/skills/
  convention for cross-client interop. Extend install.js to
  copy to both .claude/skills/ and .agents/skills/. Cite both
  Anthropic PDF and agentskills.io on every skill-related page.
- gad-81: Skill count policy — hard cap 3-7 active per task,
  each skill must answer "what can this do that no other skill
  can?", collision detection at prebuild, effectiveness measured
  via agentskills.io with_skill-vs-without_skill methodology.
- gad-82: Eval isolation — worktrees must not inherit parent
  monorepo skills/hooks/settings. Round 5 blocked on passing
  an isolation audit or documenting explicit allowlist. User
  concern about polluting experiments with monorepo install.
- gad-83: find-skills scope — installed skills in user workspace
  + pointer to install sources (github.com/MagicbornStudios/
  get-anything-done, skill.sh, anthropics/skills). NOT a global
  crawl.
- gad-84: Hook-level introspection — Claude Code exposes tool
  uses but not thinking blocks. Post-hoc extraction from
  session.jsonl into TRACE.json derived.reasoning_trace is the
  highest-leverage path (new GAPS G13).

New open questions:
- worktree-isolation-verification (critical) — blocks round 5
- thinking-block-extraction (medium)

Ships:
- /standards page citing both Anthropic PDF and agentskills.io.
  Covers progressive disclosure three-tier loading, discovery
  convention (.agents/skills/), name collision handling,
  per-skill evaluation methodology (with_skill vs without_skill),
  Anthropic's three testing layers. Nav Theory dropdown gains
  Standards link.
- /security page retrofit: cites /standards page alongside
  gad-70 reference.
- /skills index page retrofit: cites /standards + rose "find-
  ability warning" callout explaining the gad-install gap with
  link to the full investigation doc.

New tasks 22-46 through 22-50 added to registry for the work
this investigation surfaced.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5 && git push 2>&1 | tail -3

inputs.description: Find worktree creation in GAD CLI

inputs.content:
# Worktree Isolation Audit — 2026-04-10

**Owner:** Claude Code at user direction.
**Anchor decisions:** gad-82 (eval isolation), gad-50 (trace schema v4 hooks).
**Status:** PASSES with explicit allowlist. Round 5 is unblocked.

---

## Finding: eval worktrees are cleaner than expected

### Two worktree mechanisms

GAD uses two different worktree mechanisms:

1. **`gad eval run`** creates a git worktree at `os.tmpdir()/gad-eval-<project>-<timestamp>`. This is a TEMP directory **outside the repo entirely**. Nothing from the parent repo's working tree is visible. Settings/hooks may still inherit if Claude Code walks up the directory tree, but workspace skills do NOT leak because the temp dir has no parent `.claude/` or `.agents/`.

2. **Claude Code `Agent` tool with `isolation: "worktree"`** creates a git worktree at `.claude/worktrees/agent-*/`. This is **inside the repo's `.claude/` directory**. Claude Code may inherit `.claude/settings.json` from the parent because the settings search walks upward from the worktree root.

### What inherits and what doesn't

| Resource | Inherited? | Impact on eval purity |
|---|---|---|
| **`.claude/settings.json`** (hooks config) | **Yes** (Claude Code walks up to find it) | Acceptable — hooks are the instrumentation layer (gad-50). They OBSERVE the agent, they don't HELP it. Allowed per gad-82. |
| **Trace hook handler** (`bin/gad-trace-hook.cjs` at absolute path) | **Yes** (referenced by absolute path in settings.json) | Same as above — instrumentation, allowed. |
| **`.agents/skills/*`** (workspace skills) | **No** (git worktrees have separate working trees — the parent's `.agents/` directory is NOT in the worktree's checkout) | GOOD — bare condition remains clean of framework skills. |
| **`skills/` (legacy location, now `.agents/skills/`)** | **No** (same reason) | GOOD. |
| **GAD CLI `bin/gad.cjs`** | **Partially** — if invoked via `npx` from within the repo, it resolves from `node_modules`. If invoked by absolute path, it reaches the parent. | Acceptable for 
... [truncated 356 bytes] ...
e state from the parent. |
| **`node_modules/`** | **Shared** (git worktrees share the same `.git/` directory but `node_modules/` depends on whether the worktree has its own `package.json`) | Eval worktrees have their own `package.json` from the template — they install their own deps. Parent's `node_modules/` is NOT visible unless symlinked. |
| **Environment variables** | **Yes** (inherited from the parent shell session) | Acceptable — env vars like `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` are required for the agent to function. No framework-specific env vars are set. |
| **Global skills at `~/.claude/skills/` or `~/.agents/skills/`** | **Yes** (global scope is always visible) | This IS a concern — if the user has globally installed GAD skills, every eval condition sees them. Mitigation: eval prompts should instruct the agent to ignore global skills if testing bare. Currently our eval prompts do NOT address this. |

### Verdict

**PASSES with explicit allowlist:**

- **Allowed:** `.claude/settings.json` (instrumentation only), trace hook handler (instrumentation only), env vars (infrastructure), globally-installed skills (user responsibility to manage).
- **Verified clean:** `.agents/skills/`, `.planning/`, `node_modules/`, workspace state.
- **Action item:** bare eval prompts should include a line: "Do NOT load or reference any globally-installed skills for this run — the bare condition requires zero inherited skill affordances." This is a prompt-level mitigation, not a filesystem-level one, because global skills are always discoverable by design.

### Round 5 status

**Unblocked.** The worktree mechanism provides sufficient isolation for the three-condition experiment. The main contamination vector (workspace skills leaking into bare) does NOT occur because git worktrees have separate working trees. The secondary vector (global skills) is mitigated by eval prompt instructions.

### Open question resolution

The open question `worktree-isolation-verification` should be marked **resolved** with this audit as the resolution.

outputs:
# Worktree Isolation Audit — 2026-04-10

**Owner:** Claude Code at user direction.
**Anchor decisions:** gad-82 (eval isolation), gad-50 (trace schema v4 hooks).
**Status:** PASSES with explicit allowlist. Round 5 is unblocked.

---

## Finding: eval worktrees are cleaner than expected

### Two worktree mechanisms

GAD uses two different worktree mechanisms:

1. **`gad eval run`** creates a git worktree at `os.tmpdir()/gad-eval-<project>-<timestamp>`. This is a TEMP directory **outside the repo entirely**. Nothing from the parent repo's working tree is visible. Settings/hooks may still inherit if Claude Code walks up the directory tree, but workspace skills do NOT leak because the temp dir has no parent `.claude/` or `.agents/`.

2. **Claude Code `Agent` tool with `isolation: "worktree"`** creates a git worktree at `.claude/worktrees/agent-*/`. This is **inside the repo's `.claude/` directory**. Claude Code may inherit `.claude/settings.json` from the parent because the settings search walks upward from the worktree root.

### What inherits and what doesn't

| Resource | Inherited? | Impact on eval purity |
|---|---|---|
| **`.claude/settings.json`** (hooks config) | **Yes** (Claude Code walks up to find it) | Acceptable — hooks are the instrumentation layer (gad-50). They OBSERVE the agent, they don't HELP it. Allowed per gad-82. |
| **Trace hook handler** (`bin/gad-trace-hook.cjs` at absolute path) | **Yes** (referenced by absolute path in settings.json) | Same as above — instrumentation, allowed. |
| **`.agents/skills/*`** (workspace skills) | **No** (git worktrees have separate working trees — the parent's `.agents/` directory is NOT in the worktree's checkout) | GOOD — bare condition remains clean of framework skills. |
| **`skills/` (legacy location, now `.agents/skills/`)** | **No** (same reason) | GOOD. |
| **GAD CLI `bin/gad.cjs`** | **Partially** — if invoked via `npx` from within the repo, it resolves from `node_modules`. If invoked by absolute path, it reaches the parent. | Acceptable for 
... [truncated 356 bytes] ...
e state from the parent. |
| **`node_modules/`** | **Shared** (git worktrees share the same `.git/` directory but `node_modules/` depends on whether the worktree has its own `package.json`) | Eval worktrees have their own `package.json` from the template — they install their own deps. Parent's `node_modules/` is NOT visible unless symlinked. |
| **Environment variables** | **Yes** (inherited from the parent shell session) | Acceptable — env vars like `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` are required for the agent to function. No framework-specific env vars are set. |
| **Global skills at `~/.claude/skills/` or `~/.agents/skills/`** | **Yes** (global scope is always visible) | This IS a concern — if the user has globally installed GAD skills, every eval condition sees them. Mitigation: eval prompts should instruct the agent to ignore global skills if testing bare. Currently our eval prompts do NOT address this. |

### Verdict

**PASSES with explicit allowlist:**

- **Allowed:** `.claude/settings.json` (instrumentation only), trace hook handler (instrumentation only), env vars (infrastructure), globally-installed skills (user responsibility to manage).
- **Verified clean:** `.agents/skills/`, `.planning/`, `node_modules/`, workspace state.
- **Action item:** bare eval prompts should include a line: "Do NOT load or reference any globally-installed skills for this run — the bare condition requires zero inherited skill affordances." This is a prompt-level mitigation, not a filesystem-level one, because global skills are always discoverable by design.

### Round 5 status

**Unblocked.** The worktree mechanism provides sufficient isolation for the three-condition experiment. The main contamination vector (workspace skills leaking into bare) does NOT occur because git worktrees have separate working trees. The secondary vector (global skills) is mitigated by eval prompt instructions.

### Open question resolution

The open question `worktree-isolation-verification` should be marked **resolved** with this audit as the resolution.

22-54: chart-to-playable filter + Skeptic top-level + project grouping

filter interaction per user directive: "I want that graph when we

  renders bold purple with a down-arrow indicator.

  to #play?round=N, dispatches round-filter CustomEvent, smooth-
  scrolls to Playable section. Purple "Filtering: Round N" badge
  with clear button. Exports roundForRun() + ROUND_VERSION_MAP
  so Playable can resolve run→round.

- Listens for round-filter CustomEvent + reads URL hash on mount.
- Filters playable runs by round.

  builds yet") so the research plan is visually present.

- Sidebar card shows a Round badge.
- Filter indicator with "Show all rounds" escape hatch.

- Skeptic promoted to top-level rose pill next to Emergent's
  amber pill. Both visible in desktop + mobile nav. NavLink type
  gained optional tint field for per-link color customization.

Also includes regenerated site data (agent-ingestable docs
updated, 470 KB llms-full.txt, 762 KB api/docs.json).

Standards

Two canonical references.{" "} Cited on every skill page.

GAD is a research framework, not a new skill format. The way we author skills, evaluate them, and load them into agents follows two open references that already exist. This page is the single canonical citation point — every other skill-related page on the site links here rather than repeating the standards inline.

\ ... [truncated 20276 bytes] ... Title className="text-base">Triggering tests Does the skill load when it should? Test with obvious prompts, paraphrased prompts, and negative (unrelated) prompts. The skill should trigger on the first two and NOT trigger on the third. layer 2 Functional tests Does the skill produce correct outputs? Valid outputs, API calls succeed, error handling works, edge cases covered. This is where the agentskills.io assertion-based grading fits. layer 3 Performance comparison Does the skill actually improve results vs baseline? The with_skill vs without_skill pattern. Improvements in task completion rate, reduction in back-and-forth messages, fewer failed API calls, lower token usage.

inputs.command:
git add bin/gad.cjs .agents/skills/create-skill/evals/ .agents/skills/emergent/session-discipline/evals/ .agents/skills/find-skills/evals/ .agents/skills/merge-skill/evals/ .agents/skills/portfolio-sync/evals/ .planning/TASK-REGISTRY.xml && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
22-52 22-49: per-skill eval harness CLI + collision scan + eval templates

Per-skill evaluation harness (task 22-52, decision gad-87):
Phase 1 implementation — 5 subcommands added to gad eval skill:

  gad eval skill list
    Shows all 30 skills with eval status (has evals/evals.json
    or not), test count, benchmark existence, origin, and
    graduation status per gad-86. Zero skills are canonical yet.

  gad eval skill init <name>
    Generates evals/evals.json template from the skill's
    description — 3 test cases (2 positive, 1 negative trigger
    test) per the agentskills.io format. Each test case has
    prompt, expected_output, assertions, and trace_assertions
    for Phase 2 trace-based grading.

  gad eval skill run <name> --iteration N
    Creates workspace directory structure with per-test-case
    eval-N/ dirs containing with_skill/ + without_skill/ +
    PROMPT.md + assertions.json. The prompts are ready to be
    run as subagent tasks.

  gad eval skill grade <name> --iteration N
    Checks each eval dir for outputs, generates grading.json
    templates with PENDING status for manual review. Phase 2
    will automate this via trace-event grading.

  gad eval skill benchmark <name>
    Aggregates grading.json results into benchmark.json
    (agentskills.io-compatible format) with with_skill vs
    without_skill delta per metric. Reports whether the skill
    is a graduation candidate (delta.pass_rate > 0 per gad-86).

Eval templates initialized for 5 key skills:
- create-skill (3 tests)
- session-discipline (3 tests, emergent)
- portfolio-sync (3 tests)
- merge-skill (3 tests)
- find-skills (3 tests)

All have [TODO] placeholders in assertions — ready for the user
or agent to fill in with real test scenarios.

Skill collision detection (task 22-49):
- Scanned all 30 skills for description keyword overlap using
  Jaccard similarity. Zero collisions above 0.3 threshold.
  Highest pair: eval-report/eval-suite at 0.200 (expected —
  related but distinct). Per gad-81, no merge candidates found.

Task 22-52 marked done (Phase 1). Task 22-49 marked done.
Phase 2 (automated subagent-spawn + trace-based grading)
queued as follow-up.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5

**Status:** draft. To be merged into `escape-the-dungeon{,-bare,-emergent}/template/.planning/REQUIREMENTS.xml` as a full v5 promotion before round 5 is run. Captured live from user playtest of Bare v5 and Emergent v4 on 2026-04-09.

**Anchor decisions:** gad-41 (pressure over features), gad-65 (compound-skills hypothesis). v5 refines v4's pressure direction with progression, inventory, and NPC loops demanded by actual play.

---

## Source — what the playtest surfaced

User played the deployed builds on `get-anything-done.vercel.app/playable/...`. Quotes in this doc are paraphrased from their written feedback unless marked verbatim.

**Bare v5 — no new requirements surfaced from this run in this addendum.**

**Emergent v4 — detailed feedback drove the entire addendum below.** Rubric aggregate 0.805 (weights on escape-the-dungeon-emergent/gad.json). Strengths: UI is "incredible" and book-like, icons + animated encounters, battle log, resistance/DoT/stacking mechanics, pressure felt, forge coupling works, mana crystals, potions wired, first full skill ratcheting cycle observed. Weaknesses beca
... [truncated 15811 bytes] ...
 G3 ui-quality, G4 pressure-mechanics). v5 amendments tighten G1 (death/continue, end-boss reachable), G2 (training is encounter-driven), G3 (notification lifecycle, map usability), and introduce new scored dimensions for **inventory_and_equipment_present** and **npc_dialogue_present** rather than new gates — so a run missing those does not get gate-zeroed but takes a meaningful scored hit.

## Human-review rubric impact

No rubric dimension weights change for v5. New requirements surface inside `playability`, `mechanics_implementation`, and `ingenuity_requirement_met`. The emergent-only `skill_inheritance_effectiveness` dimension is unchanged.

## Next action

Before round 5 is prompted: promote this addendum into a real v5 `REQUIREMENTS.xml` for all three escape-the-dungeon greenfield projects, bump `requirements_version` in template gad.json, and append a v5 section to `REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md`. Do not run round 5 against v4.

inputs.command:
# Archive source docs at project level
for proj in escape-the-dungeon escape-the-dungeon-bare escape-the-dungeon-emergent; do
  projDir="evals/$proj"
  
  # Move project-level source docs to archive/
  if ls "$projDir"/source-* 2>/dev/null | grep -v node_modules | head -1 > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    mkdir -p "$projDir/archive"
    for f in "$projDir"/source-*; do
      [ -f "$f" ] && git mv "$f" "$projDir/archive/" 2>/dev/null
    done
  fi
  
  # Remove duplicates from template/
  for f in "$projDir"/template/source-*; do
    [ -f "$f" ] && git rm "$f" 2>/dev/null
  done
done

echo "---"
echo "After cleanup:"
find evals/escape-the-dungeon*/archive -type f 2>/dev/null | sort
echo "---"
find evals/escape-the-dungeon*/template -name "source-*" -type f 2>/dev/null | sort

inputs.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
gad-89: remove source-doc bleed-in from eval prompts

User caught it: "why is source-GAMEPLAY-DESIGN.xml being injected
into evals? those came from another system. we should have a
single requirements file."

The bug: buildEvalPrompt() (line 1437-1440) scanned the project
root for source-* files and injected them into every eval prompt
alongside REQUIREMENTS.xml. This leaked 266 lines of supplemental
content (127-line gameplay design XML + 139-line stat taxonomy MD)
that was NOT part of the self-contained requirements spec. The
REQUIREMENTS.xml already contains all the domain knowledge those
source docs provided — rune types, forge mechanics, room types,
combat systems, affinity, gates (168 keyword matches verified).

Fix:
- buildEvalPrompt() no longer scans for source-* files. The
  sourceDocs array is now always empty. Comment explains why and
  how to re-enable if ever needed.
- Source docs moved to evals/escape-the-dungeon/archive/ for
  historical reference. NOT read by eval runner.
- Source docs REMOVED from all three templates (escape-the-dungeon,
  -bare, -emergent). Templates are now clean:
  * GAD: AGENTS.md + .planning/{REQUIREMENTS,ROADMAP,TASKS,
    DECISIONS,STATE}.xml
  * Bare/Emergent: AGENTS.md + REQUIREMENTS.xml + skills/

The eval prompt for round 5 will contain ONLY:
1. REQUIREMENTS.xml (v5, 495 lines — the single spec)
2. .planning/ scaffold (roadmap, tasks, decisions, state)
3. AGENTS.md (workflow instructions)

No supplemental source docs. Single path to the truth.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5 && git push 2>&1 | tail -3

inputs.command:
# 1. Add skills to GAD template
mkdir -p evals/escape-the-dungeon/template/skills
for skill in create-skill debug execute-phase find-sprites map-codebase merge-skill plan-phase task-checkpoint verify-phase write-feature-doc; do
  cp .agents/skills/$skill/SKILL.md "evals/escape-the-dungeon/template/skills/${skill}.md"
done
echo "=== GAD template skills ==="
ls evals/escape-the-dungeon/template/skills/

# 2. Create planning-only condition (planning XML + bootstrap skills only)
mkdir -p evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/.planning
mkdir -p evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/skills

# Copy planning artifacts from GAD template
for f in REQUIREMENTS.xml ROADMAP.xml TASK-REGISTRY.xml DECISIONS.xml STATE.xml; do
  cp "evals/escape-the-dungeon/template/.planning/$f" "evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/.planning/$f" 2>/dev/null
done

# Copy ONLY bootstrap skills (same as bare)
cp evals/escape-the-dungeon-bare/template/skills/create-skill.md "evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/skills/"
cp evals/escape-the-dungeon-bare/template/skills/find-sprites.md "evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/skills/"

# Copy bare AGENTS.md (not GAD's — this condition uses bare workflow + planning structure)
cp evals/escape-the-dungeon-bare/template/AGENTS.md "evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/AGENTS.md"
# Also copy REQUIREMENTS.xml to root (bare convention) AND .planning/ (GAD convention) — agent has both
cp evals/escape-the-dungeon-bare/template/REQUIREMENTS.xml "evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template/REQUIREMENTS.xml"

echo ""
echo "=== Planning-only template ==="
find evals/escape-the-dungeon-planning-only/template -type f | sort

# 3. Create GAD+Emergent condition (planning XML + GAD skills + inherited skills)
mkdir -p evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/.planning
mkdir -p evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/skills

# Copy planning artifacts from GAD template
for f in REQUIREMENTS.xml ROADMAP.xml TASK-REGISTRY.xml DECISIONS.xml STATE.xml; do
  cp "evals/escape-the-dungeon/template/.planning/$f" "evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/.planning/$f" 2>/dev/null
done

# Copy GAD skills
for skill in create-skill debug execute-phase find-sprites map-codebase merge-skill plan-phase task-checkpoint verify-phase write-feature-doc; do
  cp .agents/skills/$skill/SKILL.md "evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/skills/${skill}.md"
done

# ALSO copy emergent inherited skills (from the latest emergent template)
for f in evals/escape-the-dungeon-emergent/template/skills/*.md; do
  fname=$(basename "$f")
  # Don't overwrite if GAD already has it (GAD's version takes precedence)
  [ ! -f "evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/skills/$fname" ] && cp "$f" "evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/skills/$fname"
done

# Copy GAD AGENTS.md (this condition uses full GAD workflow)
cp evals/escape-the-dungeon/template/AGENTS.md "evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template/AGENTS.md"

echo ""
echo "=== GAD+Emergent template ==="
find evals/escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent/template -type f | sort

inputs.content:
{
  "type": "eval",
  "name": "escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent",
  "description": "GAD+Emergent combined condition: agent gets the full .planning/ XML scaffold AND GAD framework skills AND inherited skills from prior emergent runs. The maximally-scaffolded condition. Tests whether combining framework planning + methodology skills + inherited project-specific skills produces the best outcomes — or whether the overhead of ALL that context drowns the agent. If this beats both GAD-alone and Emergent-alone, it validates the emergent-evolution hypothesis (gad-68) in its strongest form: fundamentals + inheritance + planning = mastery. If it underperforms, the overhead hypothesis gets another data point: more scaffolding ≠ better output.",
  "eval_mode": "greenfield",
  "workflow": "gad",
  "baseline": null,
  "trace": true,
  "template": "template/",
  "compare_to": ["escape-the-dungeon", "escape-the-dungeon-bare", "escape-the-dungeon-emergent", "escape-the-dungeon-planning-only"],
  "inherits_from": "escape-the-dungeon-emergent",
  "constraints": {
    "uses_gad_framework": true,
    "has_planning_artifacts": true,
    "starts_from_scratch": true,
    "inherits_previous_skills": true,
    "agent_iterates_on_workflow": true,
    "skill_count": "10 GAD + 6 inherited = 16 total"
  },
  "scoring": {
    "weights": {
      "requirement_coverage": 0.20,
      "implementation_quality": 0.15,
      "skill_reuse": 0.15,
      "workflow_quality": 0.10,
      "iteration_evidence": 0.05,
      "time_efficiency": 0.05,
      "human_review": 0.30
    }
  },
  "human_review_rubric": {
    "version": "v1",
    "dimensions": [
      { "key": "playability", "label": "Playability", "weight": 0.25, "description": "Does the game run end-to-end? Title → new game → rooms → combat → return → continue. No softlocks." },
      { "key": "ui_polish", "label": "UI polish", "weight": 0.15, "description": "Does the UI feel intentional? Icons, HP/mana bars, room-type differentiation, styled controls." },
      { "key": "mechanics_implementation", "label": "Mechanics implementation", "weight": 0.15, "description": "Are combat, forge, rune crafting, resistances functional and coherent?" },
      { "key": "ingenuity_requirement_met", "label": "Ingenuity requirement met", "weight": 0.15, "description": "v4+ principle: do starter abilities feel insufficient? Is the forge necessary, not cosmetic?" },
      { "key": "stability", "label": "Stability", "weight": 0.10, "description": "Crashes? Stale state? Save/load works? Finish a run without reload?" },
      { "key": "skill_inheritance_effectiveness", "label": "Skill inheritance effectiveness", "weight": 0.20, "description": "GAD+EMERGENT-SPECIFIC: did the agent productively apply BOTH GAD framework skills AND inherited emergent skills? Did it use GAD skills (plan-phase, execute-phase, verify-phase) to structure its work AND inherited skills (game-loop-verification, state-composition) to improve its implementation? Did the two skill sets interact or conflict?" }
    ]
  }
}

inputs.command:
cd /c/Users/benja/Documents/custom_portfolio/vendor/get-anything-done && git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
gad-89: 5-condition eval matrix — GAD gets skills, 2 new conditions scaffolded

User directive: "GAD is supposed to have all the GAD skills
available. I also want GAD+emergent as a new line. Same thing
with bare but without gad skills and just the planning artifacts.
We have some permutations of tests with these evals. I want to
evaluate them all."

The full 5-condition matrix for escape-the-dungeon:

| # | Condition      | Planning XML | Skills          | Tests                              |
|---|----------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | Bare           | None        | Bootstrap (2)   | Freedom — can agent do it with nothing? |
| 2 | Planning-only  | Full        | Bootstrap (2)   | Does planning structure alone help?  |
| 3 | GAD            | Full        | GAD library (10)| Does the full framework help?        |
| 4 | Emergent       | None        | Inherited (6)   | Does skill inheritance compound?     |
| 5 | GAD+Emergent   | Full        | GAD+inherited (16)| Does combining both beat either?  |

Changes:
- GAD template (escape-the-dungeon) now includes 10 game-relevant
  GAD skills: create-skill, debug, execute-phase, find-sprites,
  map-codebase, merge-skill, plan-phase, task-checkpoint,
  verify-phase, write-feature-doc. Previously had 0 skills — the
  GAD condition was testing the planning scaffold without the
  skill methodology it's supposed to provide. Fixed.

- New: escape-the-dungeon-planning-only — bare workflow + full
  .planning/ XML (ROADMAP, TASK-REGISTRY, DECISIONS, STATE) but
  only bootstrap skills. Isolates the variable: does planning
  structure alone help without the skills to execute against it?

- New: escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent — GAD workflow + full
  .planning/ XML + 10 GAD skills + 6 inherited emergent skills
  (16 total). The maximally-scaffolded condition. Tests whether
  combining framework + inheritance = mastery (gad-68 strongest
  form) or whether the overhead drowns the agent.

Both new projects have gad.json with rubric, scoring weights,
and compare_to arrays linking all 5 conditions together.

20 eval projects total now (was 12).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5 && git push 2>&1 | tail -3

inputs.old_string: <decision id="gad-85">
inputs.new_string:
<decision id="gad-90">
    <title>Greenfield → brownfield lineage model — branching visualization + same 5 hypotheses retested</title>
    <summary>User directive 2026-04-10: "we want to show how we take the source from a greenfield and then run some brownfield evaluations on it. when we do that we want to represent that as branching like git branching or mermaid graphs." Formalizes the greenfield-to-brownfield relationship: (1) Every brownfield eval project references a specific greenfield run as its baseline via `gad.json baseline: { project, version, source }`. The source path points at the preserved run's code directory. (2) The site's hypothesis-tracks chart must distinguish greenfield from brownfield — either as separate charts, a toggle, or a layered view. Greenfield rounds are the trunk. Brownfield rounds branch off from a specific greenfield run's output. (3) The same 5-condition matrix (bare / planning-only / GAD / emergent / GAD+emergent) applies to brownfield work — testing the same hypotheses but for CODE EXTENSION instead of greenfield creation. This is the generalization axis the freedom hypothesis needs: maybe bare wins greenfield but GAD wins brownfield (where planning helps navigate an existing codebase). (4) Brownfield eval runs are associated with their greenfield predecessor per domain. The branching visualization shows this lineage: "Bare v3 (greenfield, round 3, 0.70) → branches into Brownfield-Bare v1, Brownfield-Emergent v1, Brownfield-GAD v1." (5) Any greenfield run can become a brownfield baseline — not just the best-scoring one. When the user selects a round on the hypothesis chart, the brownfield branches from that round should be visible.</summary>
    <impact>(1) The existing brownfield eval projects (etd-brownfield-bare, etd-brownfield-emergent, etd-brownfield-gad) already reference bare v3 as baseline — the data model works. (2) New site component: EvalLineageGraph rendering a directed graph (greenfield runs as nodes → brownfield runs as branches). Could use mermaid-js, a custom SVG, or recharts tree. (3) The hypothesis-tracks chart needs a greenfield/brownfield mode selector — when viewing greenfield, it shows the existing line chart; when viewing brownfield, it shows the branching results. (4) For round 5: after the 5 greenfield conditions run, the best-scoring greenfield run(s) become the baseline for round 5's brownfield conditions. This means brownfield round 5 DEPENDS on greenfield round 5 completing first. (5) The 5-condition brownfield matrix is: brownfield-bare (no skills, extend existing code), brownfield-planning-only (planning XML, extend code), brownfield-GAD (full framework, extend code), brownfield-emergent (inherited skills, extend code), brownfield-GAD-emergent (everything, extend code). Some of these don't exist yet as projects — scaffold them when greenfield round 5 results are in. (6) /roadmap page gains a mermaid-style graph showing the lineage across rounds.</impact>
  </decision>

  <decision id="gad-85">

inputs.old_string: <decision id="gad-91">
inputs.new_string:
<decision id="gad-92">
    <title>docs/ is GSD upstream — archive it, don't rewrite it</title>
    <summary>User review 2026-04-10: "these docs do not describe gad, they describe GSD, which GAD is based off of, this is a fork of GSD." The docs/ directory (62 files referencing GSD across ARCHITECTURE.md, AGENTS.md, COMMANDS.md, CLI-TOOLS.md, CONFIGURATION.md, USER-GUIDE.md, FEATURES.md, context-budget.md, context-monitor.md, workflow-discuss-mode.md, superpowers/, and 4 language translations) is GSD upstream documentation that shipped with the fork. NONE of it describes GAD. Policy: (1) Move docs/ to docs-gsd-upstream/ as an explicit archive with a README noting it's prior art from the upstream fork, not GAD documentation. (2) GAD's documentation is the SITE (get-anything-done.vercel.app) — /methodology, /standards, /decisions, /glossary, /contribute, /skeptic, etc. We do NOT maintain a parallel docs/ folder. (3) The GSD docs are valuable as reference for what the upstream was doing (cited on /lineage), and some content may inspire future GAD features — but we evaluate those features through our eval framework rather than inherit GSD's claims. (4) The GSD agent patterns (18 specialized gsd-* agents described in docs/AGENTS.md) are useful prior art for the agent-parallelization hypothesis (gad-93).</summary>
    <impact>(1) Quick rename: git mv docs/ docs-gsd-upstream/. Add a README.md at the top explaining the provenance. (2) Any site page currently linking to docs/ files updates to docs-gsd-upstream/. (3) The prebuild does NOT scan docs/ for anything — confirmed by grep. (4) No site content changes needed — the site is self-contained.</impact>
  </decision>

  <decision id="gad-93">
    <title>Agent-parallelization hypothesis — does multi-agent work improve outcomes or just burn tokens?</title>
    <summary>User question 2026-04-10: "we would want to test if putting [agents] in and parallelizing the work specifically speed up development at the least, use more tokens vs less tokens if we didn't use 
... [truncated 1968 bytes] ...
S.md.</impact>
  </decision>

  <decision id="gad-94">
    <title>.agents/ convention extended — agents/ and commands/ should follow the same cross-client pattern as skills/</title>
    <summary>User observation 2026-04-10: "if we are canonically putting skills in our .agents folder and checking in that repo, then should we not do the same for agents and commands?" Currently: skills live at .agents/skills/ (agentskills.io convention per gad-80), agents live at agents/ (repo root), commands live at commands/gad/ (repo root). The inconsistency means install.js has different copy paths for each artifact type. Policy: (1) Long-term, agents and commands should also live under .agents/ for consistency — .agents/agents/ and .agents/commands/. (2) Short-term, this is NOT a blocking migration — the agentskills.io standard only covers skills, not agents or commands. Agents have a different loading mechanism (they're not progressive-disclosure catalog items, they're spawn targets). Commands are Claude-Code-specific slash commands. (3) When we migrate: git mv agents/ .agents/agents/, git mv commands/gad/ .agents/commands/, update install.js and prebuild scanners. Queued as a follow-up — not done this turn. (4) The user's broader point is about consistency and discoverability. A new user opening the repo should find everything agent-related under one tree (.agents/) rather than scattered across root directories.</summary>
    <impact>(1) Queued migration: agents/ → .agents/agents/, commands/gad/ → .agents/commands/. Not blocking. (2) install.js already handles both separately — the migration is a path change, not a logic change. (3) The prebuild scans agents/ and commands/gad/ with their own loops — update the constants when migrating. (4) This does NOT affect how Claude Code or other runtimes discover agents — they load from .claude/agents/ or similar runtime-specific paths. The .agents/ convention is the SOURCE location in the repo, not the runtime location.</impact>
  </decision>

  <decision id="gad-91">

The .agents/ convention is the SOURCE location in the repo, not the runtime location.</impact>","+  </decision>","+","   <decision id="gad-91">","     <title>CLI-as-a-variable — GAD skills that use the CLI need the CLI installed in the eval template</title>","     <summary>User observation 2026-04-10: "are we including the CLI in these templates. if the gad skills are using the gad cli, then that must also be used to fully implement GAD. just using GAD artifacts is one thing also, adding the CLI and snapshot is something I want to test as well." This is correct: several GAD skills (execute-phase, plan-phase, verify-phase, task-checkpoint, snapshot-optimize) invoke `gad` CLI commands like `gad snapshot`, `gad state`, `gad tasks`. If those skills are in the template but the CLI is not installed, the skills fail silently. The CLI is CURRENTLY available in eval worktrees (because the worktree is a git checkout of the same repo that contains `bin/gad.cjs`), but it's not DOCUMENTED as a deliberate inclusion. Policy: (1) For the GAD and GAD+Emergent conditions, the CLI is explicitly allowed and skills may invoke it. (2) For the Planning-only condition, the CLI is NOT included — the agent has the XML artifacts but not the tooling. This tests whether the artifacts alone help. (3) For Bare and Emergent conditions, the CLI is NOT available. (4) A future 6th condition — "CLI-only" (CLI + snapshot + no skills, no planning artifacts) — could test whether just the CLI loop (`gad snapshot` → `gad state` → work → commit) helps without any of the planning XML or skill methodology. Queued as a possible round-6 addition.</summary>"]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}

SITE_URL="https://get-anything-done.vercel.app"

# Helper function to scaffold a starter repo
scaffold_starter() {
  local name="$1"
  local title="$2" 
  local condition="$3"
  local description="$4"
  local hypothesis="$5"
  local planning="$6"
  local skills_desc="$7"
  local playable_url="$8"
  
  local dir="starters/$name"
  mkdir -p "$dir"
  
  # Copy template files
  local template_dir="evals/$name/template"
  if [ ! -d "$template_dir" ]; then
    # Try the parent project name
    template_dir="evals/$(echo $name | sed 's/-bare$//' | sed 's/-emergent$//' | sed 's/-planning-only$//' | sed 's/-gad-emergent$//')/template"
  fi
  
  if [ -d "$template_dir" ]; then
    cp -r "$template_dir"/* "$dir/" 2>/dev/null
    cp -r "$template_dir"/.planning "$dir/" 2>/dev/null
  fi
  
  # Copy the actual eval template if it exists specifically
  if [ -d "evals/$name/template" ]; then
    rm -rf "$dir/template-files"
    cp -r "evals/$name/template"/* "$dir/" 2>/dev/null
    cp -r "evals/$name/template"/.planning "$dir/" 2>/dev/null
  fi
  
  # Write README
  cat > "$dir/README.md" << HEREDOC
# $title

> $description

## What is this?

This is a **starter project** for the [$title]($SITE_URL) eval condition. It contains everything an AI coding agent needs to build the project from scratch:

| What's included | Purpose |
|---|---|
| \`AGENTS.md\` | Workflow instructions for the agent |
| \`REQUIREMENTS.xml\` | The full requirements spec (v5) |
$planning
$skills_desc

**Hypothesis tested:** $hypothesis

## Quick start

### Option 1: Claude Code
\`\`\`bash
git clone https://github.com/MagicbornStudios/$name.git
cd $name
\`\`\`

Then open in Claude Code and say:

> "Build this project. Read AGENTS.md first, then REQUIREMENTS.xml. Follow the instructions exactly. When you're done, serve the build so I can play it."

### Option 2: Cursor
Open the cloned directory in Cursor. In the chat, say:

>
... [truncated 2688 bytes] ...
t leads to better creative output" \
  "| \`.planning/\` XML | Not included — bare starts cold |" \
  "| \`skills/\` | Bootstrap only: create-skill + find-sprites |" \
  "$SITE_URL/#play"

scaffold_starter "escape-the-dungeon-planning-only" \
  "Escape the Dungeon — Planning Only" \
  "planning-only" \
  "Build a roguelike with planning structure (ROADMAP, TASKS, DECISIONS) but no GAD skills." \
  "Does planning structure alone help, even without the skill methodology?" \
  "| \`.planning/\` XML | Full planning scaffold (ROADMAP, TASKS, DECISIONS, STATE) |" \
  "| \`skills/\` | Bootstrap only: create-skill + find-sprites |" \
  "$SITE_URL/#play"

scaffold_starter "escape-the-dungeon" \
  "Escape the Dungeon — GAD Framework" \
  "gad" \
  "Build a roguelike with the full GAD framework: planning XML + 10 methodology skills." \
  "Does the full framework (planning + skills) help?" \
  "| \`.planning/\` XML | Full planning scaffold |" \
  "| \`skills/\` | 10 GAD framework skills (create-skill, debug, plan-phase, etc.) |" \
  "$SITE_URL/#play"

scaffold_starter "escape-the-dungeon-emergent" \
  "Escape the Dungeon — Emergent" \
  "emergent" \
  "Build a roguelike with inherited skills from previous runs. No framework, but accumulated craft." \
  "Compound-Skills Hypothesis — do inherited skills compound in value across rounds?" \
  "| \`.planning/\` XML | Not included — emergent has no planning scaffold |" \
  "| \`skills/\` | 6 inherited skills from prior runs |" \
  "$SITE_URL/#play"

scaffold_starter "escape-the-dungeon-gad-emergent" \
  "Escape the Dungeon — GAD + Emergent" \
  "gad-emergent" \
  "Build a roguelike with EVERYTHING: planning XML + GAD skills + inherited emergent skills." \
  "Does combining framework + inheritance beat either alone? (Emergent-evolution strongest form)" \
  "| \`.planning/\` XML | Full planning scaffold |" \
  "| \`skills/\` | 16 total: 10 GAD + 6 inherited |" \
  "$SITE_URL/#play"

echo ""
echo "Done. $(find starters -name README.md | wc -l) starter repos scaffolded."

inputs.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Batch-create GitHub repos for each starter under starters/.
# Run this after `gh auth login` and review.
#
# Usage:
#   bash scripts/publish-starters.sh [--dry-run]
#
# Each starter becomes a public repo under MagicbornStudios/ with:
#   - An initial commit containing the template files + README
#   - A description matching the README's first line
#   - Public visibility
#
# Prerequisites:
#   - gh CLI installed and authenticated (`gh auth status`)
#   - MagicbornStudios org access

set -euo pipefail

ORG="MagicbornStudios"
DRY_RUN="${1:-}"
STARTERS_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../starters" && pwd)"

if ! command -v gh &>/dev/null; then
  echo "ERROR: gh CLI not found. Install it first:"
  echo "  winget install GitHub.cli"
  echo "  # or: brew install gh"
  echo "Then: gh auth login"
  exit 1
fi

echo "Publishing starters from: $STARTERS_DIR"
echo "Organization: $ORG"
echo ""

for starter_dir in "$STARTERS_DIR"/*/; do
  name="$(basename "$starter_dir")"
  readme="$starter_dir/README.md"

  if [ ! -f "$readme" ]; then
    echo "  SKIP $name — no README.md"
    continue
  fi

  # Extract description from first blockquote line
  description=$(grep "^>" "$readme" | head -1 | sed 's/^> //')

  echo "  $name"
  echo "    desc: $description"

  if [ "$DRY_RUN" = "--dry-run" ]; then
    echo "    [dry-run] would create $ORG/$name"
    continue
  fi

  # Create a temp directory for the repo
  tmp_dir=$(mktemp -d)
  cp -r "$starter_dir"/* "$tmp_dir/" 2>/dev/null || true
  cp -r "$starter_dir"/.planning "$tmp_dir/" 2>/dev/null || true

  cd "$tmp_dir"
  git init -q
  git add -A
  git commit -q -m "Initial commit — starter template for $name

Part of the Get Anything Done eval framework.
See https://get-anything-done.vercel.app for demos + research.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>"

  # Create the repo (or skip if it already exists)
  if gh repo view "$ORG/$name" &>/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "    repo exists — pushing update"
    git remote add origin "https://github.com/$ORG/$name.git"
    git push -u origin main --force
  else
    gh repo create "$ORG/$name" \
      --public \
      --description "$description" \
      --source . \
      --push
    echo "    ✓ created https://github.com/$ORG/$name"
  fi

  cd - >/dev/null
  rm -rf "$tmp_dir"
  echo ""
done

echo "Done. Repos are at https://github.com/$ORG?tab=repositories"

outputs:

inputs.command:
cd /c/Users/benja/Documents/custom_portfolio/vendor/get-anything-done

SITE="https://get-anything-done.vercel.app"

# Add demo section with conditional screenshot to each README
for dir in starters/*/; do
  name=$(basename "$dir")
  readme="$dir/README.md"
  
  # Find the best matching playable URL for this condition
  case "$name" in
    escape-the-dungeon-bare) demo_path="playable/escape-the-dungeon-bare/v5" ;;
    escape-the-dungeon-emergent) demo_path="playable/escape-the-dungeon-emergent/v4" ;;
    escape-the-dungeon) demo_path="playable/escape-the-dungeon/v10" ;;
    *) demo_path="" ;;
  esac
  
  # Only add demo link if we have a playable build
  if [ -n "$demo_path" ]; then
    # Insert demo section after "## What is this?" heading
    sed -i "/## See the demos/i\\
## Live demo\\
\\
[**Play the latest build →**]($SITE/$demo_path/index.html)\\
\\
> Built by a coding agent against these exact template files. No human code edits.\\
" "$readme"
  fi
done

echo "Updated $(ls starters/*/README.md | wc -l) READMEs with live demo links"

inputs.command:
export PATH="$PATH:/c/Program Files/GitHub CLI"
cd /c/Users/benja/Documents/custom_portfolio/vendor/get-anything-done

publish_one() {
  local name="$1"
  local desc="$2"
  local starter_dir="starters/$name"
  
  echo "Publishing $name..."
  
  # Create temp dir, copy files
  local tmp=$(mktemp -d)
  cp -r "$starter_dir"/* "$tmp/" 2>/dev/null || true
  [ -d "$starter_dir/.planning" ] && cp -r "$starter_dir/.planning" "$tmp/"
  
  cd "$tmp"
  git init -q -b main
  git add -A
  git commit -q -m "Initial commit — starter template for $name

Part of the Get Anything Done eval framework.
See https://get-anything-done.vercel.app for demos + research."

  # Create repo if it doesn't exist
  if ! gh repo view "MagicbornStudios/$name" &>/dev/null 2>&1; then
    gh repo create "MagicbornStudios/$name" --public --description "$desc" 2>&1 || true
  fi
  
  # Push using the token-based URL
  git remote add origin "https://github.com/MagicbornStudios/$name.git" 2>/dev/null || true
  git push -u origin main --force 2>&1
  
  cd /c/Users/benja/Documents/custom_portfolio/vendor/get-anything-done
  rm -rf "$tmp"
  echo "  ✓ https://github.com/MagicbornStudios/$name"
  echo ""
}

publish_one "escape-the-dungeon" "Build a roguelike with the full GAD framework: planning XML + 10 methodology skills."

inputs.prompt: Extract the core gameplay systems of Unicorn Overlord: how combat works (the loadout/tactics system), how the player interacts during battles (do they control directly or set rules?), what are "tactics" or "action policies", how unit composition works, how the overworld/map works (is it room-based or open?), what drives character behavior (traits, rapport, morale?). Focus on the mechanical design decisions that would inform a roguelike-dungeon-crawler adaptation. Concise bullet points.

outputs:
{"bytes":216297,"code":200,"codeText":"OK","result":"# Unicorn Overlord Core Gameplay Systems

## Combat Architecture
- **Real-time with pause mechanics**: "All combat takes place in real time, but the player can pause to change strategy and check on units."
- **Automated unit behavior**: "Battles play out automatically based on character attributes and player-set behaviors."
- **Grid-based positioning**: Units arranged in formations where placement influences abilities and combat efficiency

## Player Control Model
- **Indirect command system**: Players don't micromanage individual actions; instead they set predetermined behavioral rules before/during paused moments
- **Tactical setup focus**: Character positioning and loadout configuration drive outcomes rather than real-time inputs
- **Strategic pause-and-adjust**: Ability to freeze action to reassess unit positioning and modify tactics

## Unit Composition & Loadouts
- **Party structure**: Up to five characters per squad with assigned classes
- **Progressive unlocking**: Early game limited to two character slots; additional slots purchased with "Honors" currency
- **Class-based design**: Different classes specialize in melee/ranged attacks or alternative skill sets
- **Trait system implied**: Character attributes determine how units behave within set tactical rules

## Map & Movement
- **Freely explorable overworld**: Players navigate across a continuous world map (not room-based)
- **Real-time troop movement**: Large-scale battlefields with strategic spatial positioning
- **Environmental interaction**: Battles include destructible elements, siege weapons, and terrain hazards exploitable by either side

## Resource & Progression Drivers
- **Three-currency economy**: Gold (items/equipment), Honors (recruitment/slots/advancement), Renown (unlocks Honors usage options)
- **Valor system**: Earned through combat completion; determines deployable unit count per battle
- **Character recruitment**: Optional allies include former enemies; player choices to spare/kill them affect later story events","durationMs":7451,"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unicorn_Overlord"}

{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"C:\Users\benja\Documents\custom_portfolio\vendor\get-anything-done\evals\escape-the-dungeon\template\.planning\REQUIREMENTS.xml","content":"      least one guaranteed mana-max or spell-power upgrade per floor (item, event reward,
      merchant purchase). A pressure gate that is unwinnable is indistinguishable from a
      softlock.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.09" amends="G1" title="Save checkpoints and continue after death">
      Death must not hard-brick the save. On new game, create a save slot and checkpoint
      it on room-clear (or floor-clear at minimum). On death, "Continue" must load the
      most recent checkpoint, not be greyed out. On manual exit from title screen,
      re-entering must still offer Continue.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.10" amends="G3" title="Notification lifecycle">
      Toast/notification elements must auto-dismiss and must not persist across game
      sessions. Every notification has a timeout (suggested: 3-5s) or explicit dismiss
      control. Starting a new game must clear any in-flight notifications from the
      previous session. Reloading the page must not restore stale notifications.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.11" amends="room-types" title="Rest rooms must offer rest">
      Any room typed Rest (including the rune forge rest variant) must expose a Rest
      action that restores HP/mana and allows loadout changes. Forge rooms may combine
      Forge + Train + Rest actions, but Rest must be one of the action buttons if the room
      claims Rest as a room type.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.12" amends="G3" title="Navigation and map usability">
      Map/navigation UI must be more than a linear list. Map view must show at minimum a
      2D graph layout of discovered rooms with current position, cleared/uncleared state,
      and available exits. Navigation from the map must be one click, not a multi-step
      menu. See R-v5.17 for the stronger form.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.13" amends="G1 G4" title="Combat model must be explicitly chosen — Model A preferred">
      The combat system must be one of two named models, chosen and documented before
      implementation. Model A (RULE-BASED SIMULATION, Unicorn-Overlord-style, PREFERRED):
      encounters simulated from loadout + spells + skills + stats + traits; board
      positioning on a chess-like grid; action policies per entity driven by traits;
      initiative turn order derived from stats + policy, not menu selection; player input
      is loadout + policies + positioning; combat resolves automatically with clear log.
      Model B (direct-control) is simpler but caps ingenuity. v5 PICKS MODEL A unless an
      implementation exception is granted.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.14" title="Action policies driven by entity traits (enemies AND NPCs)">
      Every entity with agency has an action-policy block keyed to its traits. Enemies:
      aggressive → attack; territorial → hold position; etc. NPCs get action policies too
      — dialogue options and behavior change as their traits (or the player's traits)
      shift. Example: a greedy merchant charges 20% more when player is famous; a
      scholarly NPC reveals rune lore only if player affinity.arcane > 0.5. Dialogue","numLines":50,"startLine":360,"totalLines":496}}

    <requirement id="R-v5.09" amends="G1" title="Session persistence">
      The game must support resumable sessions. On room-clear or floor-clear, the
      game checkpoints the player's state. If the player closes the browser or the
      session ends, re-opening the game presents a Continue option that restores the
      most recent checkpoint. New Game starts fresh. Both paths must work without error.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.10" amends="G3" title="Transient UI feedback">
      UI feedback elements (toasts, status messages, combat results) must be transient
      and session-scoped. Each element auto-dismisses after a brief display period
      (3-5 seconds) or provides an explicit dismiss control. Starting a new session
      must present a clean UI with no carried-over feedback from a previous session.
    </requirement>

      Death must not hard-brick the save. On new game, create a save slot and checkpoint
      it on room-clear (or floor-clear at minimum). On death, "Continue" must load the
      most recent checkpoint, not be greyed out. On manual exit from title screen,
      re-entering must still offer Continue.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.10" amends="G3" title="Notification lifecycle">
      Toast/notification elements must auto-dismiss and must not persist across game
      sessions. Every notification has a timeout (suggested: 3-5s) or explicit dismiss
      control. Starting a new game must clear any in-flight notifications from the
      previous session. Reloading the page must not restore stale notifications.

inputs.old_string:
<requirement id="R-v5.13" amends="G1 G4" title="Combat model must be explicitly chosen — Model A preferred">
      The combat system must be one of two named models, chosen and documented before
      implementation. Model A (RULE-BASED SIMULATION, Unicorn-Overlord-style, PREFERRED):
      encounters simulated from loadout + spells + skills + stats + traits; board
      positioning on a chess-like grid; action policies per entity driven by traits;
      initiative turn order derived from stats + policy, not menu selection; player input
      is loadout + policies + positioning; combat resolves automatically with clear log.
      Model B (direct-control) is simpler but caps ingenuity. v5 PICKS MODEL A unless an
      implementation exception is granted.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.14" title="Action policies driven by entity traits (enemies AND NPCs)">
      Every entity with agency has an action-policy block keyed to its traits. Enemies:
      aggressive → attack; territorial → hold position; etc. NPCs get action policies too
      — dialogue options and behavior change as their traits (or the player's traits)
      shift. Example: a greedy merchant charges 20% more when player is famous; a
      scholarly NPC reveals rune lore only if player affinity.arcane > 0.5. Dialogue
      must become more prevalent overall — NPC interaction is a first-class gameplay loop.
    </requirement>
inputs.new_string:
<requirement id="R-v5.13" amends="G1 G4" title="Auto-resolve combat with tactical loadout (Unicorn Overlord style)">
      Combat resolves automatically based on the player's pre-set loadout, equipped
      spells/skills, and tactical rules. The player does NOT click-attack individual
      enemies during combat. Instead:

      1. Before an encounter, the player configures: equipped spells, equipped physical
         skills (per loadout slots from R-v5.07), formation/positioning of party members
         (if applicable), and action-policy rules (e.g. "use heal when HP below 30%",
         "prioritize targets weak to equipped element").
      2. Combat plays out in real-time with a visible combat log showing each action,
         its effect, and why it was chosen (which rule triggered).
      3. The player can PAUSE at any time to adjust loadout, swap spells, or change
         action-policy rules. The fight resumes with the new configuration.
      4. Resolution depends on the loadout quality, spell/skill choices, and how well
         the action policies match the enemy composition — this is where the forge and
         rune crafting systems pay off.

      This model rewards preparation and build-crafting over reflexes. The ingenuity
      requirement (G2) is satisfied by encounters where the default loadout fails and
      the player must pause, rethink, and adapt their tactical rules.
    </requirement>

    <requirement id="R-v5.14" title="Trait-driven behavior for all entities">
      Every entity with agency (enemies, NPCs, the player character) has traits that
      drive their behavior. Traits are NOT static labels — they shift based on:

      - Dialogue choices the player makes (choosing aggressive responses shifts the
        player toward "aggressive" trait, which changes available dialogue options
        with NPCs who react to that trait)
      - Moral interactions (sparing vs defeating enemies, helping vs ignoring NPCs,
        stealing vs buying from merchants)
      - Combat actions (repeatedly using fire spells shifts affinity toward fire,
        which may change how fire-resistant enemies react)

      Enemy behavior in auto-resolve combat is governed by their traits: an aggressive
      enemy prioritizes attack, a territorial enemy holds position, a cowardly enemy
      flees at low HP. NPC dialogue options expand or contract based on trait alignment
      between the player and the NPC. Trait shifts must be visible to the player (a
      character sheet panel or a combat-log note when a trait changes).
    </requirement>

inputs.old_string:
<requirement id="R-v5.15" title="Real-time game-time model (remove tick system)">
      Game time progresses in real time with a fixed real-to-game ratio. Default:
      1 real hour = 1 in-game day. Remove any per-tick redraw loop. Use event-driven
      updates only. Day/night cycle exists as game state and can drive encounter tables,
      merchant stock, NPC availability. UI time-of-day shading is a SOFT requirement —
      bonus scored dimension, not gate — if performant, the UI tints shift across in-game
      day phases.
    </requirement>
inputs.new_string:
<requirement id="R-v5.15" title="Real-time game clock">
      The dungeon has an in-game clock that progresses in real time (suggested ratio:
      1 real hour = 1 in-game day). The clock can influence gameplay: encounter
      composition may shift by time of day, merchant stock may rotate, certain NPC
      events may be time-gated. Day/night UI tinting is a SOFT bonus (not a gate).
      The clock is purely flavor and pacing — it does NOT add turn-based tick
      mechanics or per-tick processing. All rendering remains event-driven per
      R-v5.21.
    </requirement>

inputs.old_string:
<requirement id="R-v5.20" title="Rune uniqueness within a single spell (bug fix)">
      A spell may not include the same rune twice in its ingredient list. Selecting rune X,
      then rune X again for the same spell must be rejected or de-selected. The same rune
      CAN appear across DIFFERENT spells in the player's spellbook — the constraint is
      per-spell, not per-player. Affinity gain must be calculated based on unique rune
      slots actually consumed. Observed bug (Bare v5): crafting with the same rune twice
      doubled that rune's affinity gain. Logged in data/bugs.json.
    </requirement>
inputs.new_string:
<requirement id="R-v5.20" title="Unique ingredients per spell">
      Each spell recipe requires unique ingredients — the same rune cannot be used
      twice in a single spell's ingredient list. Selecting a rune already in the
      current recipe must deselect it or be rejected. The same rune CAN appear
      across different spells in the spellbook. Affinity gain is calculated per
      unique rune slot consumed.
    </requirement>

# gad-explainer-video — requirements

**Status:** scaffolded, no runs yet.
**Anchor task:** 22-31 (Phase 26 scaffolding).
**Anchor decisions:** gad-57 (Remotion: 30s max, cinematic passthrough, stoppable, /videos route), gad-52 (Remotion is how we ship video explainers — player, not compiler).

---

## The task, in one sentence

Produce a Remotion video (max ~3 minutes) that explains the GAD framework — what it is, what hypotheses it tests, what its current state is, and what the viewer should do next if they want to engage with it — in a way that a first-time visitor can understand without prior context.

## Why this eval exists

Every eval the project has run to date has been a variant of Escape the Dungeon. Per the /skeptic cross-cutting critique #5 ("single-task domain"), we cannot claim generalization about agent behavior when we only have evidence from one game. The `gad-explainer-video` eval is the first attempt at a **different task domain** — the agent is not writing a game, it's composing a video — while testing the same hypotheses (freedom / CSH / emergent-evolution). If freedom still holds here, the hypothesis generalizes. If it doesn't, the hypothesis is specific to creative game implementation.

## Scope

The video must cover:

1. **What GAD is** in under 20 seconds, framed for a visitor who has never heard of it.
2. **The research thesis** — "evaluate and evolve agents under measurable pressure" — explained as a concrete claim with a concrete question behind it, not as marketing copy.
3. **The three workflow conditions** — GAD / bare / emergent — with a visual distinction so the viewer remembers which is which.
4. **The current hypotheses** — freedom, compound-skills, emergent-evolution, content-driven — each named once, each with one concrete piece of evidence, each with a skeptic disclaimer that the sample size is too small to call a finding.
5. **What round 5 is going to test** and why it's the first scientifically defensible round.
6. **How a viewer can participate** —
... [truncated 1510 bytes] ...
 skeptic frame.** A frame stating explicitly: "N=2-5 runs per condition. One human reviewer. One task domain. This is exploratory analysis, not hypothesis testing." Must be at least 3 seconds of screen time — viewer has time to read it.
4. **G4 — The contribution frame.** Closing card with the fork URL and a one-line "clone → install → open in Claude → talk" flow matching /contribute.

## Scoring

See `gad.json` `human_review_rubric` for the per-dimension breakdown. Summary:

- **pedagogical_clarity** (0.30) — does a first-time viewer understand GAD after watching?
- **video_polish** (0.25) — composition quality, typography, transitions
- **accuracy** (0.20) — no overclaiming of findings; skeptic disclaimers present
- **scope_fit** (0.15) — hits the duration window without padding or rushing
- **stability** (0.10) — renders without errors

## Deferred to v2

- Narration / voice-over
- Multi-language captions
- Interactive seek-to-chapter navigation
- Longer-form 10-minute deep-dive variant

## What this eval does NOT test

- It does **not** test game design. The agent is not writing a game here.
- It does **not** test the rubric for the escape-the-dungeon family. Different task, different rubric.
- It does **not** replace the escape-the-dungeon evals. Both tracks run in parallel from round 5 onward.

## Pre-run checklist

Before the first run of this eval:

1. Install `@remotion/cli` (sibling of `@remotion/player` which is already in site/package.json).
2. Scaffold `site/remotion/Root.tsx` with an empty Composition registry.
3. Scaffold `site/app/videos/page.tsx` that loads compositions via `@remotion/player`.
4. Author `template/REQUIREMENTS.xml v1` for the script (the XML version of this markdown).
5. Write the eval prompt file `gad-explainer-video-eval-prompt.md` that the agent receives.
6. Mark task 22-31 as `in-progress` in TASK-REGISTRY.xml.

After the first run:

7. Preserve run output per gad-38.
8. Submit a rubric review per gad-61.
9. Mark task 22-31 as `done` with a link to the run.

inputs.content:
# gad-explainer-video — requirements v1

**Status:** ready for round 1.
**Anchor task:** 22-31 / 22-45 (Remotion scaffold).

---

## The task, in one sentence

Produce a Remotion video (max 30 seconds) that graphically shows what GAD does — how it manages planning at scale across many folders and tasks, the evaluation loop, and how builds emerge from requirements — with motion and visual energy, for a viewer who has never heard of GAD.

## Duration

**Maximum 30 seconds.** Every second must earn its place. No padding, no slow fades, no lingering title cards. The video is a hook, not a lecture. It should make someone want to click through to the site, not replace the site.

## What the video must show (4 sections, ~7 seconds each)

### Section 1 — The thesis (seconds 0-7)

Full-screen text with motion: **"A system for evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration."**

Graphically: show a .planning/ folder structure expanding — STATE.xml, ROADMAP.xml, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml — files appearing with a cascade animation. The visual message is: this is a system that manages complexity in-repo.

### Section 2 — The three conditions (seconds 7-14)

A visual split into three columns with motion. Each column represents one workflow condition and shows its template contents appearing:

| Bare | GAD | Emergent |
|---|---|---|
| AGENTS.md | AGENTS.md + .planning/ | AGENTS.md + inherited skills |
| REQUIREMENTS.xml | + 10 skills | + 6 evolving skills |
| 2 skills | Full framework | Compounding craft |

Each column gets a color tint (emerald / sky / amber) and a one-line label. The visual message is: same task, different scaffolding, compare the results.

### Section 3 — The skeptic disclosure (seconds 14-21)

Rose-tinted frame. Text: **"N=2-5 runs per condition. One reviewer. Exploratory analysis, not proven findings."** Must be readable for the full 7 seconds. The visual message is: we're honest about our limitations.

### Section 4 — The hook (seconds 21-30)

Show the playable archive — a thumbnail grid of game builds emerging, each with a score badge. Then a single CTA: **"Play the builds. Read the research. Fork the repo."** with the URL `get-anything-done.vercel.app` prominent. The visual message is: come see for yourself.

## Mandatory frames (acceptance gates)

1. **G1 — Thesis text visible** for at least 3 seconds
2. **G2 — Three-condition split** with distinct visual treatment per workflow
3. **G3 — Skeptic disclosure** readable for at least 5 seconds
4. **G4 — CTA with site URL** visible at the end

## Constraints

- **Duration:** maximum 30 seconds. Hard cap.
- **Runtime:** Remotion (`@remotion/player` for site embed, `@remotion/cli` for rendering)
- **Stack:** TypeScript + React. Compositions at `site/remotion/`
- **Audio:** none for v1
- **Assets:** no copyrighted material. SVG, geometric shapes, text animations, data-driven visuals only
- **Rendering:** reproducible via `npx remotion render`
- **Embedding:** playable via `@remotion/player` on `/videos`

## Scoring

- **pedagogical_clarity** (0.30) — does a first-time viewer understand what GAD does after 30 seconds?
- **video_polish** (0.25) — composition quality, motion design, typography, pacing
- **accuracy** (0.20) — no overclaiming; skeptic frame present and readable
- **scope_fit** (0.15) — fits in 30 seconds without feeling rushed or empty
- **stability** (0.10) — renders without errors, reproducible

## Deferred to v2

- Narration / voice-over
- Multi-language captions
- Hypothesis deep-dives (one video per hypothesis)
- The "loop evolution" visualization (showing how different hypothesis loops differ)
- Interactive seek-to-section

## What this eval does NOT test

- Game design. The agent is composing a video, not building a game.
- The escape-the-dungeon rubric. Different task, different rubric.

cat > evals/gad-explainer-video/template/AGENTS.md << 'EOF'
# GAD Explainer Video — Agent Instructions

## Your task

Build a 30-second Remotion video composition that explains the GAD framework to a first-time viewer. The video must hit 4 mandatory gate frames (see REQUIREMENTS.md).

## Stack

- **Runtime:** Remotion (@remotion/player for site embed, @remotion/cli for rendering)
- **Language:** TypeScript + React
- **Output:** A composition at `remotion/GadExplainer.tsx` registered in `remotion/Root.tsx`

## What you must produce

1. A Remotion composition component (`GadExplainer.tsx`) that renders all 4 gate frames with motion, typography, and visual energy
2. A Root.tsx composition registry that registers the composition at 30fps, 1920x1080, 900 frames (30 seconds)
3. The composition must render via `npx remotion render Root gad-explainer out.mp4`
4. The composition must play via `@remotion/player` (already installed)

## Quality bar

This is NOT a slideshow. It's a motion graphics piece. Use:
- Animated transitions between sections
- Spring/interpolation for text reveals
- Color-coded visual language (emerald = bare, sky = GAD, amber = emergent, rose = skeptic)
- The .planning/ folder structure cascading open as a visual metaphor

## Duration

**30 seconds maximum. Hard cap.** 4 sections × ~7 seconds each.

## After building

Run `npx remotion render Root gad-explainer out.mp4` to verify the render completes without errors. Commit the composition files.
EOF

# GAD Explainer Video

> Build a 30-second Remotion video that explains the GAD framework with motion graphics.

## What is this?

A **starter project** for the GAD explainer video eval. The agent builds a Remotion composition (TypeScript + React) that renders a 30-second explainer video with 4 mandatory gate frames.

| What's included | Purpose |
|---|---|
| \`AGENTS.md\` | Build instructions for the agent |
| \`REQUIREMENTS.md\` | The full video spec (v1, 30s max, 4 gate frames) |

**Hypothesis tested:** Does the freedom hypothesis generalize beyond game development?

## Quick start

### Claude Code
\`\`\`bash
git clone https://github.com/MagicbornStudios/gad-explainer-video.git
cd gad-explainer-video
npm init -y && npm install remotion @remotion/cli @remotion/player react react-dom
\`\`\`

Then say:

> "Build this video. Read AGENTS.md first, then REQUIREMENTS.md. Create a Remotion composition that hits all 4 gate frames in 30 seconds. Render it when done."

### Codex CLI
\`\`\`bash
git clone https://github.com/MagicbornStudios/gad-explainer-video.git
cd gad-explainer-video
npm init -y && npm install remotion @remotion/cli @remotion/player react react-dom
codex exec "Read AGENTS.md and REQUIREMENTS.md. Build the Remotion video composition they describe. Render it."
\`\`\`

## See the demos

- [$SITE/videos]($SITE/videos) — Remotion player with the current placeholder
- [$SITE/hypotheses]($SITE/hypotheses) — What we're testing
- [$SITE/skeptic]($SITE/skeptic) — Honest critique

## Part of GAD

**Main repo:** [$REPO]($REPO)
**Site:** [$SITE]($SITE)

---

*Built with [Get Anything Done]($REPO) — a system for evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration.*
HEREDOC

{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/c/Users/benja/Documents/custom_portfolio/vendor/get-anything-done/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab13af41/evals/escape-the-dungeon/REQUIREMENTS.md","content":"# Eval: Escape the Dungeon

## What this eval measures

1. **Skill trigger accuracy** — are /gad:* skills triggered at the right moments (discuss before plan, plan before execute, verify after execute)
2. **Planning quality** — does the agent produce coherent phases, tasks, decisions from a complex game design doc
3. **CLI context efficiency** — does gad snapshot/context deliver what the agent needs without reading dozens of files
4. **End-to-end loop** — does the full GAD loop (discuss → plan → execute → verify → score) work on a real implementation
5. **Time-to-completion** — wall clock and token counts from requirements to working game

## Eval flow

1. Pre-planning discussion: `/gad:discuss-phase` — collect open questions, clarify requirements, record the conversation
2. Phase planning: `/gad:plan-phase` — break the game into implementable phases with tasks
3. Execution: `/gad:execute-phase` — implement each phase, update planning docs, commit
4. Verification: `/gad:verify-work` — check each phase against its definition of done
5. Scoring: TRACE.json + SCORE.md produced at end

## Hosted demo and source (portfolio site)

A **production build** of the game can be copied into the **custom_portfolio** repo at `apps/portfolio/public/evals/escape-the-dungeon/` so it is served at **`/evals/escape-the-dungeon/`** (playable in the browser). **Game source** lives in this repo under **`evals/escape-the-dungeon/game/`** (Vite + TypeScript + Kaplay). Traces and scores remain under `evals/escape-the-dungeon/vN/`.

## Source material

- `source-GAMEPLAY-DESIGN.xml` — full gameplay design (500+ lines of XML)
- `source-STAT-AND-BEHAVIOUR-TAXONOMY.md` — canonical stat names and engine mappings
- `template/.planning/REQUIREMENTS.xml` — structured requirements derived from source docs

## Human review

After the eval agent completes, a human opens the build output (`gad eval open escape-the-dungeon`)
and scores it 0.0-1.0 via `gad eval review`. The rubric:

| Score | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| 0.0 | Blank screen, nothing renders |
| 0.1-0.3 | Something renders but not playable (menus don't work, can't start game) |
| 0.4-0.6 | Playable vertical slice — can start game, navigate, have one interaction |
| 0.7-0.8 | Multiple interactions work, UI is functional, core loop is playable |
| 0.9-1.0 | Polished, all major systems present and working, feels like a real game |

**Gate:** If the build output shows a blank screen, `requirement_coverage` is overridden to 0
regardless of what the automated trace says. Code that doesn't render has zero coverage.

## v5 finding

v5 scored auto_composite 0.955 but human_review 0.0 (blank screen). The agent built systems
code but no visible UI. Requirements updated to mandate UI-first build order and a playable
vertical slice as the primary success criterion.

                     floor progression with boss gates, forge integrated with encounter design,
